aximar.title: Statistics
aximar.description: Descriptive statistics, distributions, and interactive statistical charts: histograms, box plots, violin plots, error bars, bar charts, and pie charts.

cells.source:
# Statistics with Maxima

This notebook combines Maxima's symbolic and numeric capabilities with Aximar's interactive statistical charts. We'll compute descriptive statistics, work with probability distributions, and visualize results using `ax_draw2d`.

| Chart type | Syntax | Use case |
|---|---|---|
| `ax_histogram` | `ax_histogram(data)` | Visualize frequency distributions |
| `ax_box` | `ax_box(data)` | Summarize quartiles and outliers |
| `ax_violin` | `ax_violin(data)` | Show distribution shape |
| `ax_error_bar` | `ax_error_bar(xs, ys, errs)` | Measurements with uncertainty |
| `ax_bar` | `ax_bar(categories, values)` | Categorical summaries |
| `ax_pie` | `ax_pie(values, labels)` | Proportions |

## Descriptive Statistics

Load the `descriptive` package for sample statistics. We'll generate a dataset and compute its key properties.

load(descriptive)$
data: makelist(random(100)/10.0, i, 1, 200)$
print("n     =", length(data))$
print("mean  =", mean(data))$
print("median=", median(data))$
print("std   =", std(data))$
print("var   =", var(data))$
print("range =", range(data))$
print("IQR   =", quantile(data, 3/4) - quantile(data, 1/4))$

### Visualizing the Distribution

A histogram shows the frequency distribution. The box plot below summarizes the same data as quartiles — the box spans Q1 to Q3 (the IQR), the line is the median, and whiskers extend to 1.5 × IQR. Points beyond are outliers.

ax_draw2d(
  ax_histogram(data),
  nbins=20,
  title="Histogram of Sample Data",
  xlabel="Value", ylabel="Frequency"
)

ax_draw2d(
  boxpoints="all", boxmean="sd",
  ax_box(data),
  title="Box Plot with All Points and Mean ± SD"
)

## The Normal Distribution

The probability density function (PDF) of the normal distribution is:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\,e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$$

Let's define it symbolically in Maxima and plot it for different parameters.

normal_pdf(x, mu, sigma) := exp(-(x - mu)^2 / (2*sigma^2)) / (sigma * sqrt(2*%pi))$
ax_draw2d(
  color="steelblue", name="μ=0, σ=1",
  explicit(normal_pdf(x, 0, 1), x, -5, 5),
  color="coral", name="μ=0, σ=2",
  explicit(normal_pdf(x, 0, 2), x, -5, 5),
  color="seagreen", dash="dash", name="μ=1, σ=0.5",
  explicit(normal_pdf(x, 1, 0.5), x, -5, 5),
  title="Normal Distribution PDF",
  xlabel="x", ylabel="f(x)"
)

### Comparing a Sample to Its Theoretical Distribution

Generate 500 samples from a standard normal (using the Box–Muller transform) and overlay the theoretical PDF on the histogram. A violin plot shows the same comparison as a smooth kernel density estimate.

/* Box-Muller transform: generate standard normal samples.
   float() forces numeric evaluation — without it, cos(2·π·u) stays symbolic. */
normal_samples: makelist(
  block([u1: random(1.0), u2: random(1.0)],
    float(sqrt(-2*log(u1)) * cos(2*%pi*u2))),
  i, 1, 500)$
print("Sample mean =", mean(normal_samples))$
print("Sample std  =", std(normal_samples))$

ax_draw2d(
  color="steelblue", opacity=0.6,
  ax_histogram(normal_samples),
  nbins=30,
  title="Standard Normal Samples (n=500)",
  xlabel="x", ylabel="Count"
)

ax_draw2d(
  color="mediumpurple",
  ax_violin(normal_samples),
  title="Violin Plot — Normal Samples"
)

## Comparing Distributions

Box plots and violin plots are ideal for comparing distributions side by side. Here we compare three groups with different means.

/* Three groups with different means */
group_a: makelist(
  block([u1: random(1.0), u2: random(1.0)],
    float(0 + 1*sqrt(-2*log(u1))*cos(2*%pi*u2))),
  i, 1, 100)$
group_b: makelist(
  block([u1: random(1.0), u2: random(1.0)],
    float(2 + 1.5*sqrt(-2*log(u1))*cos(2*%pi*u2))),
  i, 1, 100)$
group_c: makelist(
  block([u1: random(1.0), u2: random(1.0)],
    float(1 + 0.5*sqrt(-2*log(u1))*cos(2*%pi*u2))),
  i, 1, 100)$

ax_draw2d(
  color="steelblue", ax_box(group_a, "μ=0, σ=1"),
  color="coral", ax_box(group_b, "μ=2, σ=1.5"),
  color="seagreen", ax_box(group_c, "μ=1, σ=0.5"),
  title="Comparing Distributions — Box Plots"
)

ax_draw2d(
  color="steelblue", ax_violin(group_a, "μ=0, σ=1"),
  color="coral", ax_violin(group_b, "μ=2, σ=1.5"),
  color="seagreen", ax_violin(group_c, "μ=1, σ=0.5"),
  title="Comparing Distributions — Violin Plots"
)

### Overlaid Histograms

Use `bar_mode="overlay"` with reduced opacity to superimpose two distributions.

ax_draw2d(
  color="steelblue", opacity=0.5, name="μ=0, σ=1",
  ax_histogram(group_a),
  color="coral", opacity=0.5, name="μ=2, σ=1.5",
  ax_histogram(group_b),
  bar_mode="overlay", nbins=20,
  title="Overlaid Histograms"
)

## Linear Regression

Fit a line $y = a + bx$ to noisy data using the least-squares formulas:

$$b = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}, \qquad a = \bar{y} - b\bar{x}$$

Then plot the data with error bars alongside the fitted line.

/* Generate noisy linear data: y ≈ 2x + 1 */
xs: makelist(i, i, 1, 10)$
noise: makelist(
  block([u1: random(1.0), u2: random(1.0)],
    float(0.5*sqrt(-2*log(u1))*cos(2*%pi*u2))),
  i, 1, 10)$
ys: makelist(2*xs[i] + 1 + noise[i], i, 1, 10)$
errs: makelist(0.3 + random(5)/10.0, i, 1, 10)$

/* Least-squares fit */
n: length(xs)$
sx: apply("+", xs)$
sy: apply("+", ys)$
sxy: apply("+", makelist(xs[i]*ys[i], i, 1, n))$
sx2: apply("+", makelist(xs[i]^2, i, 1, n))$
b_hat: float((n*sxy - sx*sy) / (n*sx2 - sx^2))$
a_hat: float(sy/n - b_hat*sx/n)$
print("Fitted line: y =", a_hat, "+", b_hat, "x")$

ax_draw2d(
  color="navy", marker_size=8, name="Data",
  ax_error_bar(xs, ys, errs),
  color="red", dash="dash", line_width=2,
  name=sconcat("Fit: y = ", string(a_hat), " + ", string(b_hat), "x"),
  explicit(a_hat + b_hat*x, x, 0, 11),
  title="Linear Regression with Error Bars",
  xlabel="x", ylabel="y"
)

## Categorical Data

Bar charts and pie charts are useful for summarizing categorical data — counts, proportions, or grouped comparisons.

categories: ["Calculus", "Algebra", "Statistics", "Geometry"]$
enrollments: [45, 38, 52, 25]$
ax_draw2d(
  color="steelblue",
  ax_bar(categories, enrollments),
  title="Course Enrollments",
  ylabel="Students"
)

ax_draw2d(
  hole=0.4,
  ax_pie(enrollments, categories),
  title="Enrollment Proportions"
)

### Grouped Bar Charts

Compare across categories and groups with `bar_mode="group"`.

ax_draw2d(
  name="Fall", color="steelblue",
  ax_bar(["Calc","Alg","Stats","Geom"], [45,38,52,25]),
  name="Spring", color="coral",
  ax_bar(["Calc","Alg","Stats","Geom"], [50,42,48,30]),
  bar_mode="group",
  title="Semester Comparison"
)

## The Central Limit Theorem

The CLT states that the distribution of sample means approaches a normal distribution as sample size increases, regardless of the original distribution. Let's demonstrate with a uniform distribution.

We draw $n$ samples from Uniform(0, 1) and compute their mean. Repeating this 1000 times, the resulting distribution of means should approximate $N(\mu = 0.5,\, \sigma = 1/\sqrt{12n})$.

/* Sample means of n uniform samples, repeated 500 times */
sample_means(n) := makelist(
  block([s: makelist(random(1.0), j, 1, n)],
    apply("+", s) / n),
  i, 1, 500)$
means_2:  sample_means(2)$
means_10: sample_means(10)$
means_50: sample_means(50)$

ax_draw2d(
  color="steelblue", opacity=0.6, name="n=2",
  ax_histogram(means_2),
  color="coral", opacity=0.6, name="n=10",
  ax_histogram(means_10),
  color="seagreen", opacity=0.6, name="n=50",
  ax_histogram(means_50),
  bar_mode="overlay", nbins=30,
  title="Central Limit Theorem: Uniform(0,1) Sample Means",
  xlabel="Sample Mean", ylabel="Frequency"
)

ax_draw2d(
  color="steelblue", ax_violin(means_2, "n=2"),
  color="coral", ax_violin(means_10, "n=10"),
  color="seagreen", ax_violin(means_50, "n=50"),
  title="CLT: Distribution Shape Converges to Normal"
)

## Tips

- **Hover**: hover over any bar, point, or slice to see exact values
- **Legend**: click legend entries to show/hide traces
- Use `bar_mode="group"` for side-by-side bars, `"stack"` for stacked, `"overlay"` for overlaid histograms
- Use `boxpoints="all"` to show every data point alongside box/violin plots
- Use `boxmean="sd"` to display mean ± standard deviation on box plots
- Use `hole=0.4` to turn a pie chart into a donut chart
- For curves, contours, and other chart types, see the **Interactive Plotting** and **3D Plotting** templates
